items.mistake: Defining the state too richly (or too sparsely).
items.fix: The state should be the smallest tuple of parameters such that two same-state sub-problems are interchangeable. Add a dimension only when you actually need it.
items.why: Too-rich states blow up time/space; too-sparse states make the recurrence wrong.
items.example:
```python
# Coin change min coins: state = `target` is enough.
# Adding the number of coins used so far would be redundant — same target ⇒ same future.
```

items.fix: Compute sub-problems in dependency order — every state must already exist when you read it.
items.why: Reading an uncomputed cell silently uses 0 (or garbage) and produces a wrong answer.
items.example:
```python
# Knapsack: outer loop over items, inner loop over capacity.
for i in range(1, n + 1):
    for c in range(capacity + 1): ...
```

items.fix: Initialize the smallest sub-problems explicitly before the loop or check them in the recursion.
items.why: Without `dp[0]` (or `dp[0][*]` / `dp[*][0]`) set correctly, the entire table is wrong.
items.example:
```python
dp[0] = 0    # base case
for t in range(1, target + 1): ...
```

items.mistake: Reaching for DP when sub-problems don't overlap.
items.fix: If you'd never solve the same sub-problem twice, plain divide-and-conquer (e.g. mergesort) is the right call.
items.why: Adding a memo table costs memory and complexity with no payoff.
items.example:
```python
# Mergesort splits don't overlap — no benefit from memoization.
```

items.mistake: Forgetting to compress space when the recurrence allows it.
items.fix: If `dp[i]` depends only on `dp[i-1]`, keep two rows (or roll a single array).
items.why: Full tables can be O(m·n) memory; many real workloads fit only with rolling rows.
items.example:
```python
prev = [...]
for i in 1..m:
    curr = [...]
    # fill curr from prev
    prev = curr
```
